figures.gloss: A daughter of the Jade Emperor exiled to earth for breaking a jade cup, who lived, died, and returned through successive human lives before being enthroned as chief of the Mother Goddesses. One of Vietnam's Four Immortals, her seat is the Phủ Dầy temple complex in Nam Định.
figures.facet: She is no gentle madonna: the legends make her willful, punishing, and combative — a goddess who extracts recognition from emperors and monks, canonized from below like a popular verdict the state had to ratify.
figures.provenance: Đoàn Thị Điểm, 'Vân Cát thần nữ truyện' (1730s); Olga Dror, 'Cult, Culture, and Authority' (2007); Ngô Đức Thịnh, 'Đạo Mẫu ở Việt Nam'.

figures.gloss: The cosmos divided into four color-coded courts — Heaven (red), Earth (yellow), Water (white), and Mountains-and-Forests (green) — each under a Mother Goddess and staffed by ranked mandarins, dames, princes, princesses, and boy attendants.
figures.facet: It is a celestial administration built on the imperial court's model but crowned by women — the bureaucratic pantheon of the Sinosphere with Mothers, not an Emperor, as its operative sovereigns.
figures.provenance: Ngô Đức Thịnh, 'Đạo Mẫu ở Việt Nam'; Kirsten Endres, 'Performing the Divine' (2011).

figures.gloss: The central ritual, also called hầu bóng, 'serving the shadows': in a fixed sequence of possession episodes the spirits of the Four Palaces descend in turn into a medium (thanh đồng), each incarnation marked by costume change, dance, and sung invitation, and sealed by the distribution of blessed gifts (lộc).
figures.facet: Possession as repertory theatre with real stakes — dozens of named spirits in ranked order, each with its own dress, gestures, and music. Banned as superstition in the socialist North after 1954, it returned with Đổi Mới to become the crown of the 2016 UNESCO inscription.
figures.provenance: Barley Norton, 'Songs for the Spirits' (2009); Kirsten Endres, 'Performing the Divine' (2011); UNESCO ICH inscription, 'Practices related to the Việt beliefs in the Mother Goddesses of Three Realms' (2016).

figures.gloss: The historical general who repelled the Mongol invasions of the thirteenth century, deified after his death in 1300 as a supreme exorcist-saint. His martial Trần-dynasty court of spirits is honored alongside the Four Palaces, and mediums possessed by him drive out malevolent spirits.
figures.facet: He shows the national hero and the god as one office: state commemoration and spirit possession run on the same figure, letting the religion carry Vietnamese patriotism inside its pantheon.
figures.provenance: Phạm Quỳnh Phương, 'Hero and Deity: Trần Hưng Đạo and the Resurgence of Popular Religion in Vietnam' (2009); temple traditions of Kiếp Bạc.

figures.gloss: The ritual song genre that summons and serves the spirits during lên đồng: moon-lute, percussion, and a singer whose verses praise each descending spirit's legend, landscape, and rank, cueing the medium's every transformation.
figures.facet: The music is the liturgy's engine — spirits are sung down into bodies by named verse, so precisely that the songs double as the pantheon's oral archive.
figures.provenance: Barley Norton, 'Songs for the Spirits' (2009); recognized within the 2016 UNESCO inscription.

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: Two women deified from below into supreme popular goddesses of their nations, both forcing state ratification of their cults; in southern Vietnam, Mazu (Thiên Hậu) worship interweaves with the Mother cult it parallels.
GOLD SEAM: Both project the imperial bureaucracy into the spirit world — ranked celestial offices mirroring the earthly court — though the Vietnamese version answers to Mothers.
GOLD SEAM: Her legend makes her the Jade Emperor's exiled daughter — the Taoist celestial court adopted wholesale as the Mother's family tree.

GOLD SEAM: Two modern Vietnamese revelation technologies in mirror image — Đạo Mẫu brings the spirits down into ecstatic, costumed bodies; Caodaism replaced exactly that trance with sober bureaucratic spirit-writing.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme Mother presiding beside and outside the male administrative order — Caodaism's Jade Pool Mother draws on the same Vietnamese mother-goddess current that Đạo Mẫu crowns.
GOLD SEAM: East Asian professional mediumship as staged sequence — the Korean gut and Vietnamese lên đồng both run ranked spirits through one specialist's body with music, costume, and fee.
GOLD SEAM: The mounted body across hemispheres — the deity rides a human vehicle whose own personality withdraws for the duration.
Intra-tradition: Liễu Hạnh reigns as Mother of the Heavenly Palace, the sovereign apex of the Four-Palace order.
Intra-tradition: the songs are the rite's ignition — no spirit descends unsung.

dossier.essence: Vietnam's indigenous Mother Goddess religion: the worship of Thánh Mẫu Liễu Hạnh and the Mothers of the celestial palaces, practiced through temple networks and spirit mediums rather than a centralized church, strongest in the northern deltas with offshoots throughout the country and the diaspora. Its formative cult rose around Liễu Hạnh at Phủ Dầy (Nam Định) on an older Vietnamese goddess substrate; suppressed as superstition in the socialist North after 1954, it revived spectacularly after Đổi Mới (1986), and its Mother Goddess practices were inscribed by UNESCO in 2016. Its central claim is practical: the Mothers and their courts, properly served, dispense health, fortune, and protection in this life.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmos is a celestial administration modeled on the imperial court but crowned by women: the palaces of Heaven, Water, and Mountains-and-Forests (the Three Realms; a fourth, Earth, completes the Tứ Phủ scheme), each under a Mother Goddess and staffed by ranked mandarins, dames, princes, and princesses — many of them deified historical figures such as Trần Hưng Đạo. These spirits remain active in the world and descend into human bodies when properly invited. There is no elaborated salvation doctrine; the dead persist within the ancestral order, and the religion's energy is directed at efficacy in the present life rather than at the next.
dossier.practice: The central rite is lên đồng (hầu bóng): a medium (thanh đồng), called to service through affliction and initiated by 'opening the palaces' (mở phủ), incarnates a fixed sequence of spirits, each descent marked by costume change, dance, and the chầu văn songs of a ritual ensemble, and sealed by distributing blessed gifts (lộc) to the participants who sponsor and attend. Temple festivals — above all the Phủ Dầy festival in the third lunar month — organize the cult's calendar; the practice is open to men and women alike, demands substantial ritual expenditure, and has flourished alongside Vietnam's market economy.
dossier.canon: There is no scripture; the corpus is oral and performed — the chầu văn song repertoire, spirit legends and temple hagiographies (thần tích), with Đoàn Thị Điểm's 'Vân Cát thần nữ truyện' (1730s) as the earliest literary account of Liễu Hạnh.
dossier.reflects: Religion as efficacious performance — the divine made verifiably present through costumed, musical embodiment, and asked above all for this-worldly fortune.
sources: Olga Dror, 'Cult, Culture, and Authority: Princess Liễu Hạnh in Vietnamese History' (2007)
Barley Norton, 'Songs for the Spirits: Music and Mediums in Modern Vietnam' (2009)
Kirsten W. Endres, 'Performing the Divine: Mediums, Markets and Modernity in Urban Vietnam' (2011)
UNESCO, 'Practices related to the Việt beliefs in the Mother Goddesses of Three Realms' (2016)

links.cite: Barley Norton, 'Songs for the Spirits: Music and Mediums in Modern Vietnam' (2009)
